---
title: "24 · Sarsa: TD for Action Values, On-Policy"
subtitle: "TD(0) for q(s,a) using transitions (s,a,r,s',a') from the policy being learned."
lesson-id: "mfrl.sarsa"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.td-state-values"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "drafted"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Can you state the TD(0) update for $v(s)$? — see [MFRL — 23, TD(0)](../23-td-state-values/index.qmd).
2. Do you remember what an *action value* $q_\pi(s, a)$ measures versus a
   *state value* $v_\pi(s)$? — see [MFRL — 08, Action Values](../08-action-values/index.qmd).
3. Can you describe ε-greedy action selection? — see [MFRL — 19, ε-Greedy](../19-epsilon-greedy/index.qmd).
4. Do you know what "on-policy" suggests by name?

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.

:::

## Touch

Below is a 4×4 grid. The agent starts at the top-left. Moving onto the bottom-
right cell ($G$) gives reward $+1$ and ends the episode; every other step
gives reward $-0.04$. The agent acts ε-greedy on its current $q$-table.
$\gamma = 0.9$.

The widget runs **Sarsa**: at every step, after observing $(s, a, r, s', a')$,
update $q(s, a)$ toward $r + \gamma q(s', a')$. Watch the $q$-table heatmap.

```{ojs}
viewof sarsaEpsilon = Inputs.range([0.0, 0.5], {value: 0.1, step: 0.01, label: "ε ="})
viewof sarsaAlpha = Inputs.range([0.05, 0.5], {value: 0.2, step: 0.01, label: "α ="})
viewof sarsaEpisodes = Inputs.range([10, 1500], {value: 300, step: 10, label: "Episodes:"})
viewof sarsaSeed = Inputs.range([1, 30], {value: 6, step: 1, label: "Seed:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  function mulberry32(a) {
    return function() {
      a |= 0; a = a + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
      let t = a;
      t = Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 15, t | 1);
      t ^= t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, t | 61);
      return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    };
  }
  const rand = mulberry32(sarsaSeed * 13);
  const N = 4;          // 4x4 grid; goal at (3,3)
  const A = 4;          // 0=up,1=down,2=left,3=right
  const gamma = 0.9;
  const q = new Array(N * N * A).fill(0);
  function idx(r, c, a) { return (r * N + c) * A + a; }
  function step(r, c, a) {
    let nr = r, nc = c;
    if (a === 0) nr = Math.max(0, r - 1);
    if (a === 1) nr = Math.min(N - 1, r + 1);
    if (a === 2) nc = Math.max(0, c - 1);
    if (a === 3) nc = Math.min(N - 1, c + 1);
    const reward = (nr === N - 1 && nc === N - 1) ? 1 : -0.04;
    const done = (nr === N - 1 && nc === N - 1);
    return {nr, nc, reward, done};
  }
  function chooseAction(r, c) {
    if (rand() < sarsaEpsilon) return Math.floor(rand() * A);
    let best = 0, bestQ = -Infinity;
    for (let a = 0; a < A; a++) {
      const v = q[idx(r, c, a)];
      if (v > bestQ) { bestQ = v; best = a; }
    }
    return best;
  }
  for (let ep = 0; ep < sarsaEpisodes; ep++) {
    let r = 0, c = 0;
    let a = chooseAction(r, c);
    for (let t = 0; t < 200; t++) {
      const {nr, nc, reward, done} = step(r, c, a);
      const ap = chooseAction(nr, nc);
      const target = done ? reward : reward + gamma * q[idx(nr, nc, ap)];
      q[idx(r, c, a)] += sarsaAlpha * (target - q[idx(r, c, a)]);
      r = nr; c = nc; a = ap;
      if (done) break;
    }
  }
  // Build a heatmap of max_a q(r,c,a)
  const cells = [];
  for (let r = 0; r < N; r++) for (let c = 0; c < N; c++) {
    let v = -Infinity;
    for (let a = 0; a < A; a++) v = Math.max(v, q[idx(r, c, a)]);
    cells.push({r, c, v});
  }
  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return Plot.plot({
    width: 360, height: 360,
    color: {scheme: "viridis", legend: true, label: "max_a q(s,a)"},
    x: {label: "col"}, y: {label: "row", reverse: true},
    marks: [
      Plot.cell(cells, {x: "c", y: "r", fill: "v"}),
      Plot.text(cells, {x: "c", y: "r", text: d => d.v.toFixed(2), fill: "white"})
    ]
  });
}
```

The bright cells near the goal are the high-value states. Sarsa learns the
$q$ of *the policy it is actually following* — including the ε-randomness.

## Wonder

1. Sarsa updates $q(s, a)$ toward $r + \gamma q(s', a')$. Why is the *action
   actually taken* at $s'$ — not the greedy action — used in the target?
2. If we made $a'$ the greedy action even when we acted ε-greedy, what would
   change about what $q$ converges to?
3. Sarsa learns the $q$ of the policy that *generated the data*. So the more
   exploratory you are, the more "noise-aware" the learned policy. Why is
   that a feature?
4. What is the right way to *use* a Sarsa-learned $q$? Act greedy on it?
   Keep ε-acting on it? Decay ε to zero?

## Discover

Six problems. By the end you'll have derived Sarsa from first principles
and know exactly what it converges to.

### Problem 1 — why $q$, not $v$?

Suppose you have an exact value function $v_\pi$. To act *greedily* with
respect to $v_\pi$ at state $s$, you need $\arg\max_a \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma v_\pi(s') \mid s, a]$. What's the obstacle?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You need a *model* — the transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — to
take that expectation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
You'd need the full MDP transitions $P(s'\mid s,a)$ and rewards $R(s,a)$.
**Action values $q_\pi(s, a)$ remove that need:** $\arg\max_a q_\pi(s, a)$
selects the best action without any model. So if you intend to act greedily
*model-free*, you must learn $q$, not $v$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Bellman for action values

Write the Bellman equation for $q_\pi(s, a)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same shape as for $v$, but with one more layer of expectation over $a'$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[r + \gamma \, q_\pi(s', a') \,\big|\, s, a\right],$$
where the expectation is over $r$, $s'$ (from the dynamics) and $a' \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s')$ (from the policy).
</details>

### Problem 3 — the Sarsa target

Apply the same trick as TD(0): substitute the bootstrapped current estimate
for the unknown $q_\pi$ in the Bellman equation. Write the target.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Sarsa target:** $r + \gamma \, q(s', a')$, where $a' \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s')$
is the *action actually taken next* under the policy. Update:
$$q(s, a) \leftarrow q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma q(s', a') - q(s, a)].$$
</details>

### Problem 4 — why "Sarsa"?

Look at the data tuple needed for one Sarsa update. Spell out its name.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(s, a, r, s', a')$ — five things. **S–A–R–S–A.** That's the name.
</details>

### Problem 5 — on-policy

Sarsa updates $q(s, a)$ using the action $a' \sim \pi$ — the *same* policy
that chose $a$. Why does that mean Sarsa learns $q_\pi$ for whichever $\pi$
generates the data?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The Bellman equation it's solving has $a' \sim \pi$ inside the expectation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The expectation $\mathbb{E}_\pi[r + \gamma q_\pi(s', a')]$ is computed by
*sampling* $a' \sim \pi$. So the algorithm only ever moves toward $q_\pi$
where $\pi$ is the data-generating policy. Acting and learning use the same
$\pi$ — that's the definition of **[on-policy](#on-policy-glossary)**.

If you act ε-greedy with respect to the current $q$, then $\pi$ is the
ε-greedy policy, and Sarsa learns $q_\pi$ for that ε-greedy $\pi$ — *not*
$q^\*$. To get an optimal policy you must additionally drive $\varepsilon \to 0$, which is a *policy improvement* step.
</details>

### Problem 6 — convergence

State a sufficient condition for tabular Sarsa to converge to $q^\*$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Standard RM step conditions, plus enough exploration, plus the policy must
become greedy in the limit.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sufficient conditions (Singh, Jaakkola, Littman, Szepesvári 2000):
(i) every $(s, a)$ visited infinitely often;
(ii) $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ per $(s, a)$;
(iii) the policy is **GLIE** — Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration:
$\pi_t \to \pi^\*$ while continuing to visit every action infinitely often.
A common GLIE choice: ε-greedy with $\varepsilon_t = 1/t$.
Then $q \to q^\*$ a.s., and the policy converges to $\pi^\*$.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
Sarsa is TD(0) for action values — same algebra, one extra index. The
difference that *matters* is the source of $a'$: Sarsa samples it from the
data-generating policy, so the algorithm learns $q_\pi$ rather than $q^\*$.
On-policy is a *consequence* of the target, not a separate design choice.
:::

::: {.fun-fact}
**Sarsa** was introduced by **Rummery & Niranjan** in their 1994 Cambridge
technical report under the name *Modified Connectionist Q-Learning* — a
mouthful that nobody used. **Rich Sutton** renamed it *Sarsa* in passing,
in his 1996 book draft, because the data tuple $(s, a, r, s', a')$ spells
the name out. The rename stuck; the original name is forgotten outside
historians.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Sarsa — on-policy TD(0) for action values.** Given a sample tuple
$(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})$ collected under policy $\pi$,
update
$$
q(s_t, a_t) \;\leftarrow\; q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha_t \, [\,\underbrace{r_{t+1} + \gamma q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})}_{\text{Sarsa target}} - q(s_t, a_t)\,].
$$
$\pi$ is usually ε-greedy with respect to the current $q$. Under GLIE step
sizes and exploration, $q \to q^\*$ a.s. and $\pi \to \pi^\*$.

**On-policy** []{#on-policy-glossary} = the policy used to generate data
is the same as the policy being evaluated/improved.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *State, action, reward, state, action — the five.*
> *Bootstrap from the action you actually took.*
> *Learn the value of who you really are —*
> *Including the noise that explores.*

## Apply

### Worked example — one Sarsa step on a cliff-walk

> A grid: $s_0 \to s_1$ has reward $-1$; $s_1 \to s_2$ has reward $+10$ (goal,
> terminal). $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$. Initial $q$-table is all zero.
> The agent acts ε-greedy with $\varepsilon = 0.1$. Suppose the trajectory is
> $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2$ with greedy actions $a_0 = a_1 = \text{right}$.
> Compute the Sarsa updates.

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. **Step 1:** $(s_0, a_0=\text{right}, r=-1, s_1, a_1=\text{right})$.
   Target $= -1 + 0.9 \cdot q(s_1, \text{right}) = -1 + 0 = -1$.
   $q(s_0, \text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot (-1 - 0) = -0.5$.
2. **Step 2:** $(s_1, a_1=\text{right}, r=+10, s_2, \text{terminal})$.
   Target $= 10 + 0 = 10$ (terminal next).
   $q(s_1, \text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot (10 - 0) = 5.0$.

After one episode: $q(s_0, \text{right}) = -0.5$, $q(s_1, \text{right}) = 5.0$.
Subsequent episodes will pull $q(s_0, \text{right})$ toward
$-1 + 0.9 \cdot 5 = 3.5$ — the reward propagates back one state per episode,
exactly as in TD(0).

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Distinguish $q_\pi$ from $q^\*$ in one sentence each.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$q_\pi(s, a)$: expected return after taking $a$ in $s$ and *then following
policy $\pi$* forever. $q^\*(s, a)$: same, but *then following the optimal
policy* forever. Sarsa estimates $q_\pi$ for the data-generating $\pi$;
Q-learning (Lesson 26) estimates $q^\*$.
</details>

**W2.** Compute one Sarsa update with $r = 0$, $\gamma = 0.95$, $\alpha = 0.1$,
$q(s, a) = 4$, $q(s', a') = 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Target $= 0 + 0.95 \cdot 5 = 4.75$. TD error $= 4.75 - 4 = 0.75$.
$q(s, a)_{\text{new}} = 4 + 0.1 \cdot 0.75 = 4.075$.
</details>

**W3.** True or false: "Sarsa with $\varepsilon = 0$ converges to $q^\*$."

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**False.** $\varepsilon = 0$ means greedy acting from the start. The agent
will never visit some $(s, a)$ pairs; condition (i) of the convergence
theorem fails. Without exploration, Sarsa locks onto a sub-optimal greedy
policy.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that **Expected Sarsa** — the variant that uses
$r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a'\mid s') q(s', a')$ as target — has *lower*
variance than Sarsa.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sarsa samples one $a'$; Expected Sarsa averages over all $a'$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sarsa's target has variance from the random $a'$. Expected Sarsa replaces
this random sample with its expectation, killing that source of variance:
$\mathrm{Var}_{\text{ESarsa}} = \mathrm{Var}_{\text{Sarsa}} - \mathrm{Var}_{a' \sim \pi}[\gamma q(s', a')]$. Lower variance ⇒ usually faster convergence.
We expand Expected Sarsa in [Lesson 27](../27-td-unified-view/index.qmd).
</details>

**P2.** A 2-state MDP: $s_1$ has actions {left, right}. Right gives reward
0 and goes to $s_2$ (terminal, reward $+1$). Left gives reward $-1$ and
self-loops. $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $\varepsilon = 0.5$. Initial
$q = 0$. Run one episode where the agent takes right then terminates.
Compute the update.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Tuple: $(s_1, \text{right}, r=0, s_2, \text{terminal})$.
Target $= 0 + 0 = 0$ (terminal next).
$q(s_1, \text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot (0 - 0) = 0$. **No update!**
The agent didn't see the reward at the goal because the terminal target
is bootstrapped to zero. **Lesson:** the reward signal must arrive at the
*last non-terminal* transition, where the target sees it. Some textbook
formulations use $r$ from the *transition into* the terminal — using that
convention, target $= 1 + 0 = 1$, $q(s_1, \text{right}) = 0.5$. Convention
matters; pick one and stay consistent.
</details>

**P3.** Show that Sarsa is a Dvoretzky-shaped iteration (per state-action
pair). Identify $\alpha_k$, $\beta_k$, $T_k$, $w^\*$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Repeat the Lesson-21 P3 argument for $q$ instead of $v$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$q(s, a)_{k+1} = (1 - \alpha) q(s, a)_k + \alpha [r + \gamma q(s', a')_k]$.
So $\alpha_k = \beta_k = \alpha$, $T_k = r + \gamma q(s', a')_k$,
$w^\* = q_\pi(s, a)$. The expected-Bellman operator is a $\gamma$-contraction;
Dvoretzky's contracting-target form delivers convergence.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Why is Sarsa preferred over Q-learning for safety-critical
control?*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sarsa learns the value of the policy *with its exploration noise included*.
Q-learning learns the value of the optimal policy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Consider a robot near a cliff with ε-greedy exploration. Q-learning learns
$q^\*$ — the *cliff-edge optimal* path. Sarsa learns $q_\pi$ for the
ε-greedy policy — which *includes* the random falling-off-the-cliff actions
in its target. So the Sarsa-optimal policy will *avoid* the cliff edge,
because falling off is a real cost it sees. This is the *cliff-walking*
example from Sutton & Barto (Example 6.6) and is the canonical argument
for on-policy methods in robotics and finance.
</details>

**S2.** Sarsa's data tuple has 5 things; Q-learning's has 4 (you'll see in
Lesson 26). What's the missing one and why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Q-learning needs $(s, a, r, s')$ — no $a'$, because the target uses
$\max_{a'} q(s', a')$ rather than the action actually taken. So Q-learning
is **off-policy** (data and target policies can differ). Sarsa requires the
$a'$ because its target depends on it; that requirement *forces* Sarsa to be
on-policy.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Sarsa converges to $q^\*$."** Only with GLIE exploration. Otherwise it
   converges to $q_\pi$ for the data-generating $\pi$ — which may be
   sub-optimal.
2. **"Sarsa needs the $a'$ to be greedy."** Wrong — Sarsa needs $a'$ to be
   sampled from $\pi$, *whatever* $\pi$ is. If your $\pi$ is ε-greedy then
   $a'$ may be random.
3. **"Sarsa is just Q-learning with a weaker target."** No — they target
   different things. Sarsa targets $q_\pi$ (whatever you do); Q-learning
   targets $q^\*$ (the best). Different fixed points.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else:

1. **Sarsa update:** $q(s, a) \leftarrow q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma q(s', a') - q(s, a)]$ with $a' \sim \pi$.
2. **On-policy.** Sarsa learns the value of *the policy that produced the
   data*. Including ε-noise in your behaviour means you learn the value of
   a noisy policy.
3. **GLIE for $q^\*$.** Decay ε to zero (with infinite exploration along
   the way) to converge to $q^\*$.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you derive the Sarsa update from the action-value Bellman equation,
   without notes?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without peeking past
   *Hint 1*?
3. Can you state, in one breath, why Sarsa is on-policy?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Lesson 25 — n-Step Sarsa](../25-n-step-sarsa/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S1 — the cliff-walking intuition is what every
  RL practitioner cites.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read Problem 5. The "$a' \sim \pi$" step is what makes
  Sarsa Sarsa.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[MFRL — 23, TD(0)](../23-td-state-values/index.qmd)** — the
  state-value version we generalise.
- **[MFRL — 08, Action Values](../08-action-values/index.qmd)** — the $q_\pi$
  function we are now estimating.
- **[MFRL — 19, ε-Greedy](../19-epsilon-greedy/index.qmd)** — the data-
  generating policy.

### What this unlocks

- **[MFRL — 25, n-Step Sarsa](../25-n-step-sarsa/index.qmd)** — bootstrapping
  after $n$ steps instead of one.
- **[MFRL — 26, Q-Learning](../26-q-learning/index.qmd)** — the off-policy
  cousin.
- **[MFRL — 27, TD Methods Unified](../27-td-unified-view/index.qmd)** —
  Sarsa, Q, Expected Sarsa as targets in the same formula.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** On-policy actor-critic methods (A2C, A3C, PPO) all evaluate
their *current* policy with Sarsa-style targets and improve from there.
PPO's value-function loss is mechanically a Sarsa-like update on a
neural-network estimator.
See [Bellman equation](/glossary.qmd#bellman-equation).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Source:** Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement
  Learning* (Springer 2025), Chapter 7 §7.2. Cited freely; never transcribed.
- **Touchstones honoured:** Russian school problem-first sequencing,
  Better Explained "name from the tuple" framing, Bhāskara verse for the
  Sarsa step.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (widget, recursion, English, grid) ✓, graduated hints ✓, worked
  solutions ✓, common-confusions ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
